title: Event Sourcing
description: Persist domain events as source of truth; rebuild state via replay; strong auditability and temporal queries.

cost.costNotes: Seeded via heuristics; validate against current pricing and expected workload (throughput, storage, retention, egress).

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Event sourcing requires persistent event store infrastructure with high write throughput and substantial storage for event history. Minimum ($100): Small-scale implementation using managed database (e.g., AWS RDS PostgreSQL t3.small ~$50/month) plus additional storage for event logs (~$30/month) and snapshot storage (~$20/month). Maximum ($5000): Production-grade system with dedicated event store (e.g., EventStoreDB cluster ~$1500), high-IOPS storage for event streams (~$1000), snapshot/projection databases (~$800), message queue/streaming infrastructure for event publishing (Kafka/Kinesis ~$500-1000), increased storage costs due to append-only nature and retention requirements (~$500), and bandwidth for event replay operations (~$200-700). Costs scale with event volume, retention period, number of projections, and replay frequency.
provenance.adoptionCost: Event sourcing is one of the most complex architectural patterns to implement correctly. High adoption cost factors include: (1) Significant paradigm shift from CRUD/state-based thinking to event-driven modeling requiring team training (~$2000); (2) Complex implementation of event store, versioning strategy, and replay mechanisms (~$3000); (3) Building projection/read model infrastructure and keeping them synchronized (~$1500); (4) Implementing event schema evolution and migration strategies (~$1000); (5) Testing temporal queries, replay scenarios, and eventual consistency edge cases (~$500). Requires deep understanding of domain-driven design, distributed systems, and eventual consistency. Despite AI coding assistants helping with boilerplate, the architectural decisions and domain modeling complexity remain high. Expected 3-6 weeks of focused development for experienced team, longer for teams new to pattern.
provenance.licenseCost: Event sourcing is an architectural pattern with excellent open-source implementations. No inherent license cost required. Open-source event stores available include EventStoreDB (free open-source version), Apache Kafka, PostgreSQL, or purpose-built solutions like Axon Framework (open-source). Commercial options exist (EventStoreDB Cloud, Axon Server Enterprise) but are optional. Most organizations can implement event sourcing entirely with open-source tools. However, organizations may optionally purchase commercial event store licenses ($5000-20000/year) or enterprise support contracts for production deployments, but these are operational costs, not required licenses for adopting the pattern itself.

provides.reasoning: Event sourcing inherently provides complete audit trails by persisting all domain events as immutable records, enabling full traceability of all state changes over time.
Event sourcing enables temporal queries by allowing state reconstruction at any point in time through event replay, providing historical state views and time-travel capabilities.
Event sourcing fundamentally provides the ability to replay events to rebuild state, recover from failures, or create read models.
The pattern enables rebuilding current state from scratch by replaying the event log, which is core to this pattern.
Event sourcing requires and implements an event store — an append-only log of domain events that serves as the system of record.

requires.reasoning: Event sourcing requires a specialized append-only event store to persist domain events durably and in order, serving as the source of truth for the system.

As events are immutable and persist long-term, event sourcing requires a strategy to handle schema evolution (versioning, upcasting) to accommodate changing business requirements without breaking event replay.
Events must be serialized for storage and deserialized for replay, requiring a consistent serialization mechanism (JSON, Avro, Protobuf, etc.).

supports_nfr.reason: Event sourcing can achieve up to 99.99% availability with properly configured event stores and replay mechanisms.
Event sourcing requires at least 100ms p95 latency due to event appending overhead and serialization.
Event sourcing p99 latency starts at 1000ms due to tail latencies in event store writes and schema evolution.
Event sourcing requires at least 5 minutes RTO to replay events and rebuild application state from event store.
Event sourcing supports up to 100K jobs/hour with optimized event stores and asynchronous event processing pipelines.
Event sourcing read queries are constrained by event replay and projection rebuilding overhead at high QPS.
Event sourcing write throughput is limited by event store append performance and serialization overhead.
Event sourcing adds complexity (event store, replay, projections). At <5 writes/s, simpler CRUD patterns are more appropriate.
Event sourcing inherently provides complete audit logging through immutable event streams but can work with or without external audit systems.
Event sourcing supports all tenant isolation strategies by partitioning event streams and projections per tenant.
Event sourcing can handle both PII and non-PII data with appropriate encryption and pseudonymization strategies for events.
Event sourcing supports GDPR compliance with additional mechanisms for right-to-erasure through event encryption or tombstoning.
Event sourcing supports HIPAA compliance through immutable audit trails, temporal queries, and encrypted event storage.
Event sourcing supports CCPA requirements with right-to-know through event replay and right-to-delete through crypto-shredding.
Event sourcing naturally supports SOX compliance through immutable event logs, complete audit trails, and temporal state reconstruction.
Event sourcing supports read-your-writes consistency as applications can read from event streams immediately after appending events.
Event sourcing provides strict durability through immutable event logs that are persisted before acknowledgment to clients.

supports_constraints.reason: Event sourcing is a cloud-agnostic architectural pattern that can be implemented on any cloud provider or on-premises infrastructure.
Event sourcing is a language-agnostic architectural pattern implementable in any programming language with event serialization support.
Event sourcing applies to backend systems that handle domain events and state reconstruction across multiple platform types.
Event sourcing works with or without caching, though caching can optimize read-heavy projections and snapshot retrieval.
Event sourcing requires asynchronous messaging infrastructure to publish and consume domain events across bounded contexts.
Event sourcing is compatible with AI inference workloads and can provide temporal training data through event replay.
Event sourcing supports multi-tenancy by partitioning event streams per tenant with isolated event stores and projections.
Event sourcing works with batch processing for analytics and can replay historical events for batch computation.
Event sourcing can work with distributed transactions through saga patterns or operate without them using eventual consistency.
Event sourcing naturally supports real-time streaming as events are published to event streams immediately upon persistence.
Event sourcing is compatible with vector search by projecting events into vector embeddings for similarity queries.
Event sourcing can use document stores for event storage or projections, but also works with relational event stores.
Event sourcing can use key-value stores for snapshots and projections to optimize read performance and state reconstruction.
Event sourcing can project events into graph databases for relationship queries and temporal graph analysis.
Event sourcing naturally aligns with time-series databases as events are temporally ordered and immutable by design.
Event sourcing is primarily designed for transactional OLTP systems that capture and persist domain events as state changes.
Event sourcing supports OLAP workloads by replaying events for analytical queries and building read-optimized projections.

event_store_type.description: Storage mechanism for events. Append-only-log (Kafka, EventStore) for high throughput, event-stream for real-time processing, database-table for simpler deployments with ACID guarantees.

snapshot_strategy.description: How to optimize state reconstruction. None replays all events (simple but slow), periodic creates snapshots at intervals, on-demand snapshots on read, hybrid combines both for performance.

snapshot_frequency.description: Number of events between snapshots when using periodic strategy. Lower values reduce replay time but increase storage; higher values save storage but slow reconstruction.

event_versioning.description: Strategy for evolving event schemas. Upcasting transforms old events to new format on read, multiple-versions maintains handlers for each version, weak-schema uses flexible formats like JSON.

consistency_model.description: Consistency guarantee for read models. Strong consistency ensures immediate visibility (slower, simpler), eventual consistency allows stale reads (faster, requires handling delays).
